author: m.kalinin
content: what can go wrong if bids are allowed to be submitted by any version of a builer? how can this be exploited?

author: tersec
content: this entire exercise of planning for an entirely abstract maybe-what-if and adding this complexity when it's currently useless new potential attack surface at best is questionable. If we want to plan for (to use <@592004585506340885>'s example) mandatory proofs, fine, that's worth working out the logistics of. But <@755590043632140352>'s notion of, just plan in an open handwavy way for whatever, let's just see what happens, remains dubious

author: tersec
content: Ethereum already has enough never-used jank

author: tersec
content: to give an example of how that changes reasoning: really concretely, how likely are mandatory proofs in specifically Hegota? I'd currently rank it as on the... low side. Which means we have plenty of time, as Hegotá develops, to watch how I* takes form, and see if there really is something in I* which needs this kind of anticipation. But aim at known targets, not the mist

author: vladimir_ea
content:
It makes sense if the builder trusts the proposer, if they don't rely on trust, the builder would do well to observe p2p instead. 

Also protecting against 'same slot' unbundling is somewhat meaningless, as I am sure you are aware transactions are not tied to slots and any transaction released in slot N that does not land on chain can be applied unbundled in slot N+1.

author: potuz
content: There's no trust here? If the builder gets a signed block at second 1 and broadcast it itself and hasn't seen an equivocation at second 3, looking at attestations is overkill. And yes, builders that want to be protected and not leak any info unless they're sure the block is canonical will need to wait for attestations. This was always the case

author: vladimir_ea
content:
so:
low bar: receive the signed block and then observe that the same block is the only one on p2p
high bar: consume attestations to ensure block is canonical
?

author: potuz
content: Yes, equivocations should always be seen cause they could have been broadcast earlier, the builder is guaranteed not to pay if an equivocation was seen. Attestations are stronger,.

author: huaifeng0666

Hi <@755590043632140352> , our internal AI auditing tool flagged a potential issue in Prysm’s ePBS implementation. We did an initial pass over the report, and the finding looks plausible, but I’d like to get your view on it.

The potential impact could be a consensus split if the report is correct. Full write-up here:
https://notes.ethereum.org/ewmJoTTGRQGUCfR2T8G10w (ethereum account sign-in required)

author: potuz
content: did it need the same batch to reuse the index and add a deposit for an existing builder?

author: terencechain
content:
it needs to be swept too. Like
Builder list is [x balance 0]
Deposit requests is 1. y-> valid sig 2.  x-> invalid sig
The result should be [y]
but our result is [y, x]

author: terencechain
content:
This would be a good consensus spec test <@592004585506340885> 

  - Pre-state: builder V sits at index 0, fully withdrawn — withdrawable_epoch <= current_epoch and balance == 0, so its slot is reusable.
  - Request 1: brand-new pubkey A, valid signature → registers at index 0, evicting V's pubkey from the registry.
  - Request 2: V's pubkey with a junk signature.
  - The bug: Prysm treats request 2 as a top-up (V was still registered when the batch was scanned) and skipped its signature. At apply time V is gone, so it took the new-builder path with sigValid=true never checked → appended V as a new validator with junk signature

author: jtraglia
content: Yeah that sounds like a good one. I'll merge it before the release is made if you can make the test, confirm it finds the issue in prysm, then make a PR for it.

author: potuz
content: yeah but I thought swept builders would also be removed from the state, perhaps when sweeping a builder we should probably also remove the pubkey or similar. Anyway, if this is actually possible to be triggered definitely a good spectests

<@755590043632140352> <@586161934425128960> <@504202741933932544> <@654267572107083777> an alternative which only adds the reserved list:
https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/pull/5435

author: potuz
content: ahh ok, checked this is indeed applicable, depositor has to time the deposit precisely to when the builder is swept. nice find. we should probably remove the pubkey from the slice when the builder is withdrawn

author: nflaig
content: so if you change `PAYLOAD_BUILDER_VERSION` to something like `PAYLOAD_BUILDER_MAX_VERSION` and do a `<=` it would be essentially same as the PR from Potuz?

author: jtraglia
content:
Close but not quite.
* (1) His allows version 0 and 1 to submit bids, this just allows version 0.
* (2) His allows 0xB0 and greater whereas this only allows 0xB0-0xBF.

author: nflaig
content: (1) but I mean if you do `PAYLOAD_BUILDER_MAX_VERSION = 1`

author: jtraglia
content: But sure, if `PAYLOAD_BUILDER_MAX_VERSION` were 0 it would behave like that.

content: Sure, yeah.
